course_info.title: Ethics in Digital Communications
prerequisites: DC201 - Social Media Strategy and Management

learning_objectives: Apply established ethical frameworks (utilitarianism, deontology, virtue ethics) to digital communications dilemmas
Identify the unique ethical challenges created by digital technologies and platforms
Analyze the professional codes of ethics from PRSA, AMA, and IAB
readings: Parsons, Ethics in Public Relations, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital communications professionals wield significant influence over public perception, consumer behavior, and information ecosystems. This course develops your ethical reasoning skills so you can navigate the complex moral landscape of persuasion, data collection, transparency, and truth in digital spaces.
key_concepts: Ethical frameworks: utilitarianism (greatest good), deontology (duty-based rules), virtue ethics (character-based)
The Potter Box: a four-step ethical reasoning model for communications professionals
Digital-specific ethical challenges: algorithmic manipulation, data exploitation, deepfakes, dark patterns
Professional codes: PRSA, AMA, IAB, SPJ ethical standards and how they apply to digital practice
Ethical vs. legal: many legal practices are unethical, and ethical behavior may exceed legal requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Applying the Potter Box to three real-world digital ethics cases: a brand using dark patterns to prevent unsubscribing, an influencer promoting a product they do not use, and a political campaign using microtargeted disinformation
setup_requirements: Professional codes of ethics (provided on LMS)

review.value: A four-step ethical analysis framework: (1) Define the situation factually, (2) Identify the values in tension, (3) Apply ethical principles, (4) Choose loyalties. It does not prescribe an answer but structures the reasoning process.

User interface designs that manipulate users into unintended actions (hidden unsubscribe buttons, confusing opt-out language, shame-based decline options); legally questionable and ethically indefensible regardless of legality

learning_objectives: Evaluate the ethical implications of data collection practices beyond legal compliance
Design consent experiences that are genuinely informed rather than performatively compliant
Analyze the tension between personalization benefits and surveillance costs
readings: Zuboff, The Age of Surveillance Capitalism, Ch. 1-3 (selected excerpts)
Electronic Frontier Foundation, 'Privacy and Digital Rights' overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Just because you can collect data does not mean you should. The gap between what data collection laws permit and what ethical practice demands is wide, and digital communicators must understand both the power and the responsibility that come with access to personal data.
key_concepts: Surveillance capitalism: the monetization of personal data and behavioral prediction as a business model
Informed consent vs. performative consent: meaningful choice vs. GDPR-compliant but deceptive cookie banners
Data minimization as ethical practice: collecting only what you need, retaining only as long as necessary
The personalization paradox: users want relevant experiences but distrust the data collection that enables them
Children and vulnerable populations: heightened ethical obligations in data collection and targeting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethical audit of five real consent experiences (cookie banners, email opt-ins, app permissions), scoring each on genuine informed consent vs. manipulative compliance theater

review.value: Zuboff's concept describing the extraction and monetization of personal behavioral data by technology companies, creating prediction products that anticipate and influence human behavior, often without meaningful consent or awareness

Consent mechanisms that technically comply with regulations while being designed to maximize data collection through confusing language, dark patterns, and pre-selected options that undermine genuine informed choice

class_schedule.topic: Misinformation, Disinformation, and Truth in Communications
learning_objectives: Distinguish between misinformation, disinformation, and malinformation
Evaluate a communicator's ethical responsibility to verify information before amplifying it
Develop strategies for combating misinformation without amplifying it
readings: Wardle & Derakhshan, 'Information Disorder' (Council of Europe report)

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital communicators are nodes in information networks. Every piece of content you create or share can either strengthen or weaken the information ecosystem. Understanding how false information spreads and how to responsibly verify before amplifying is an ethical imperative.
key_concepts: Information disorder taxonomy: misinformation (false, no harmful intent), disinformation (false, intentional), malinformation (true but used to harm)
The amplification responsibility: how sharing unverified information makes you complicit in its spread
Verification techniques: reverse image search, source checking, claim cross-referencing, metadata analysis
The backfire effect: why correcting misinformation can sometimes reinforce it
Platform responsibility vs. individual responsibility in combating information disorder
lecture_notes.demonstration: Verification workshop: students receive five viral social media claims and must use verification tools to determine which are true, false, or misleading within a 20-minute time constraint
setup_requirements: Verification toolkit: Google Reverse Image Search, Snopes, fact-checking sites

review.value: The ethical obligation to verify information before sharing, commenting on, or incorporating it into professional communications; amplifying false information, even unintentionally, contributes to information disorder

The psychological phenomenon where correcting a false belief can sometimes strengthen it, because the correction repeats and reinforces the false claim; effective debunking leads with the truth rather than repeating the myth

class_schedule.topic: AI, Deepfakes, and Synthetic Media Ethics
learning_objectives: Evaluate the ethical implications of AI-generated content in professional communications
Identify deepfakes and synthetic media and assess their potential for harm
Develop ethical guidelines for AI use in communications practice
readings: Chesney & Citron, 'Deepfakes and the New Disinformation War' (Foreign Affairs)
Anthropic, 'Responsible AI Use in Professional Contexts' guide

lecture_notes.introduction: AI can now generate text, images, audio, and video that are indistinguishable from human-created content. This capability creates extraordinary efficiency and extraordinary ethical risk. Communications professionals must develop frameworks for responsible AI use before the technology outpaces the ethics.
key_concepts: Generative AI in communications: content creation, personalization, research assistance, and their ethical boundaries
Deepfakes and synthetic media: AI-generated video and audio that fabricate events or statements
Disclosure obligations: when and how to disclose AI involvement in content creation
Authenticity and trust: how AI content affects audience trust in communications
AI bias: how training data biases propagate into AI-generated communications content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating five content samples: determining which are human-created and which are AI-generated, then discussing the ethical implications if each were published without disclosure in different professional contexts

review.value: The ethical obligation to disclose when AI has been used substantively in content creation; the threshold for disclosure and the appropriate method vary by context, but the default should be transparency

AI systems trained on biased data produce biased outputs; when communications professionals use AI for audience targeting, content generation, or sentiment analysis, existing biases can be amplified at scale without human oversight

learning_objectives: Apply ethical frameworks to resolve ambiguous real-world communications dilemmas
Develop a personal ethical framework for professional practice
Navigate organizational pressure to engage in unethical communications practices
readings: Kidder, How Good People Make Tough Choices, Ch. 1-4
Selected whistleblower accounts from digital communications industry (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Real ethical dilemmas are not between right and wrong. They are between right and right, or between two wrongs when you must choose the lesser one. This session prepares you for the messy reality of ethical decision-making when your employer, client, or industry norm conflicts with your ethical principles.
key_concepts: Right vs. right dilemmas: truth vs. loyalty, individual vs. community, short-term vs. long-term, justice vs. mercy
Organizational pressure: when your boss asks you to do something unethical, what are your options?
Ethical courage: the willingness to speak up, refuse, or resign when ethical lines are crossed
Building an ethical framework: defining your personal non-negotiables and how to apply them under pressure
Industry ethics evolution: how norms change over time and the role of professionals in driving that change
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethical dilemma simulations: students receive progressively more complex scenarios (employer asks to suppress negative reviews, client wants to target vulnerable populations, algorithm reveals discriminatory outcomes) and must make and defend real-time decisions
setup_requirements: Professional code of ethics for reference

review.value: The most challenging ethical situations where both options have legitimate moral claims: telling the truth may harm someone you are loyal to, serving the individual may disadvantage the community; these require frameworks, not intuition

The professional willingness to raise concerns, refuse unethical directives, and accept personal consequences for maintaining ethical standards; it is a skill that can be developed, not just a personality trait

questions.question: Describe the four steps of the Potter Box and apply it to this scenario: your employer asks you to write a glowing product review to post anonymously on Amazon.
questions.answer: Step 1 - Define the situation: The employer wants you to create fake consumer testimony (astroturfing) to artificially boost product credibility. Step 2 - Identify values: honesty/transparency vs. loyalty to employer, consumer trust vs. competitive pressure. Step 3 - Apply principles: utilitarianism (deception harms the information ecosystem and all consumers), deontology (lying is wrong regardless of outcome), virtue ethics (what kind of professional do you want to be?). Step 4 - Choose loyalties: loyalty to the public's right to authentic information outweighs loyalty to the employer's short-term sales goal. Conclusion: decline the request and explain that astroturfing violates FTC guidelines and professional ethics.

rubric.key: Applies all four Potter Box steps correctly
Reaches and defends a clear conclusion

questions.question: Explain the difference between informed consent and what this course calls 'consent theater.' Provide one specific example of consent theater in digital communications.
questions.answer: Informed consent means the individual genuinely understands what data is being collected, how it will be used, and has a real, easy choice to decline without penalty. Consent theater is technically compliant consent mechanisms designed to maximize data collection while minimizing actual understanding: the letter of the law without its spirit. Example: a cookie banner with a prominent green 'Accept All' button and a tiny gray 'Manage Preferences' link that leads to a complex multi-page settings interface where each category must be individually toggled off. This technically offers choice but is designed to make acceptance the only practical option.

questions.question: Explain the backfire effect and describe a strategy for correcting misinformation that avoids triggering it.
questions.answer: The backfire effect occurs when attempts to correct a false belief actually strengthen it, because the correction repeats the false claim and reinforces it in memory. Strategy to avoid it: lead with the truth rather than the myth. Instead of saying 'It is not true that vaccines cause autism,' which repeats and reinforces the false claim, say 'Vaccines are safe and effective, as confirmed by extensive research involving millions of children.' The truth-first approach gives the audience the correct information as the primary message, mentioning the myth only briefly and only after establishing the factual narrative.

questions.question: Under what circumstances should a communications professional disclose the use of AI in content creation, and what are the risks of non-disclosure?
questions.answer: Disclosure is ethically required when: (1) AI generated substantive content (articles, images, videos) that audiences would assume was human-created. (2) AI was used in targeting or personalization in ways that could influence decisions (health, finance, politics). (3) The context requires authenticity (testimonials, reviews, expert opinions). Risks of non-disclosure: (1) Trust erosion if the audience discovers AI involvement after the fact. (2) Legal liability if AI-generated claims are inaccurate and the audience believed them to be expert human opinion. (3) Industry reputation damage as non-disclosure normalizes deceptive practices. (4) AI bias propagation without accountability when no human is identified as responsible for the content.

major_assignments.title: Ethics Audit and Personal Ethics Framework
major_assignments.description: Conduct an ethics audit of a real organization's digital communications practices, evaluating data collection, content authenticity, targeting practices, and transparency. Then develop a personal professional ethics framework that defines your non-negotiables and decision-making approach.
objectives: Audit an organization's digital communications for ethical practices and concerns
Apply ethical frameworks to identify and analyze ethical issues
Develop a personal professional ethics framework for digital communications practice
Propose specific ethical improvements with implementation recommendations
deliverables: Ethics audit report (8-10 pages) covering data practices, content authenticity, targeting, and transparency
Analysis of three specific ethical issues found, each evaluated using the Potter Box
Personal ethics framework (3-4 pages) with non-negotiables, decision process, and scenario applications
Recommendations for ethical improvements with implementation plan

criteria: Audit covers data collection, content practices, targeting, and transparency comprehensively
Ethical issues are identified with specific evidence
Analysis goes beyond surface-level observation to structural critique
Competitive comparison provides context for ethical assessment
Potter Box is applied rigorously to each identified issue
Multiple ethical perspectives are considered before reaching conclusions
Analysis acknowledges complexity rather than defaulting to simple right/wrong
Recommendations are practical and specific
Framework articulates clear non-negotiable principles
Decision-making process is structured and applicable to ambiguous situations
Framework is tested against realistic scenarios with convincing application
Framework demonstrates genuine personal reflection rather than generic platitudes
Writing is clear, precise, and well-organized
Sources are properly cited
Tone is appropriate for professional ethics discussion
Deliverables are complete and submitted on time
Thorough ethics audit demonstrating analytical rigor
Sophisticated ethical analysis using established frameworks
Genuine personal ethics framework tested against real scenarios
Professional presentation suitable for organizational adoption

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on ethical frameworks and digital ethics concepts

weekly_case_analyses.description: Weekly ethical case study analyses with written arguments

major_assignment.description: Ethics audit and personal ethics framework

midterm_examination.description: Case study examination requiring ethical analysis and reasoning

final_examination.description: Comprehensive ethics examination with live dilemma resolution

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all sessions. Ethical discussions require participation. More than 2 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Case analyses submitted late miss the peer discussion component.
course_policies.collaboration: Ethical discussions are collaborative and encouraged. All written deliverables are individual. Academic integrity in an ethics course is held to the highest standard.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Irony is not lost on us: academic dishonesty in an ethics course results in automatic failure. All work must be original and all sources cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need internet access for research and a laptop for in-class writing. Verification tools and ethics resources are accessed online.
